output_text:
Cleaned-up revision of the abstract, with light tightening of prose, removal of redundancies, and small precision fixes.

---

Type-and-effect systems extend conventional type systems by tracking computational effects—state mutation, exceptions, non-determinism—in types. The classical formulation, due to Lucassen and Gifford, has been extended in many directions; two prominent contemporary approaches are effect handlers, which model effects as algebraic operations, and capability-based systems, which track effect access through linearly typed permissions.

We develop a row-polymorphic effect calculus, Linot, that accommodates both handlers and capabilities under a single typing discipline. Linot is parameterized by a row of effect labels and uses a novel form of row subtyping that supports effect inheritance and capability narrowing. We prove preservation, progress, and effect soundness.

Our key technical insight is that capability-based effect tracking can be encoded as a row constraint with linear cardinality. This encoding retains the static guarantees of capability systems while inheriting the compositional reasoning of handler-based ones, clarifying the relationship between the two design directions.

We give a contextual operational semantics, prove the metatheory via a logical relation, and provide an OCaml implementation that exercises the approach on examples involving exceptions and mutable references.

---

Changes made:
- Removed parenthetical asides ("(which model effects as…)") and folded them into the main clause for better flow.
- Cut "In this work" and "We believe" as filler.
- Replaced "operational semantics in the style of contextual operational semantics" (redundant) with "contextual operational semantics."
- Tightened "a small implementation … that demonstrates the approach on a few example programs" to "an OCaml implementation that exercises the approach on examples."
- Standardized to four paragraphs with clearer roles: background, contribution, insight, artifacts.

No factual claims were altered; only phrasing.
